consisted of three boats, similar to whale boats, and was made by

himself. They were clinker built, some fifteen feet long, and carried three men each: the hunter on the front seat, with three rifles and a paddle; a hand in midship with a paddle; and a steersman behind, with a paddle and a mast and sail to use when desired. We took provisions and ammunition in the boats.
as hunter in one boat, I in another, and a man by the name of
in in [sic] the third, we coasted from

Our mode of procedure was this: We traveled by sail or paddle to a place where otters were known to be found, and waited for a favorable day. Very few spots suitable for camps were found. There must be fresh water, and a place where the boats can land through the surf without capsizing. We often ventured to land at spots where it was risky. This we did by approaching and waiting for a big roller; then, by vigorous strokes—the steersman keeping her bow straight on—we would go in on top of it, and be sent so far up as to give time to unload and carry boat and all up out of the reach of succeeding billows.

The otter were found lying on the kelp with only the head in sight, and always in bunches, or schools. They are shot in the head, and can best be hunted when the water is still. Approaching noiselessly and placing our boats so that the otter were in the center, we would begin the battle, loading and firing rapidly until the otter were all scattered. Then, picking up our game, we would give chase to one of the runaways. Here the difficulty was in keeping track of the game, as they travel under the water and only come to the surface for breath. The hunter has seen the direction of the dive and paddles in that direction, ready to fire when the otter's head comes above the surface. The chase is kept up until the otter is killed or the trail lost; then if no other otter can be started, the hunt is up; and as soon as skinning, fleecing, and drying is through with, a travel is made to another locality.

Otter hunting is a very exciting business, and somewhat dangerous, as you are apt to think when you hear a bullet whistle past you, it having glanced from a wave as it came from a hunter's gun opposite, who had shot at an otter on a line between you and him. But these risks will be run for an otter skin, worth thirty dollars, and especially when there is rivalry among the hunters.

, joined in with us, making, with

, five boats. The advantage in having a vessel was that with water and provisions on board the hunters could be lowered at any point, the vessel cruising around until we got through, and in this way places inaccessible for boats could be hunted.

, then, otter giving out, we abandoned the hunt and beat back against headwinds to

. Here a general division and setlting up was effected, and I found myself with forty dollars in money and five or six otter skins, a broken arm, and my feet so badly bruised that I could scarcely walk on crutches.
My physical condition happened in this way:
to hunt, if favorable weather intervened, while she made further search below for otter. The weather continuing boisterous, part of us set out to traverse

on foot. Soon after we left camp I, in order to get a shot at some spotted seals, ventured too near the brink of a projecting point. My feet flew up, I could find nothing to grasp, and I went over. But I yelled, gave my gun a cast that it might fall flat, and straightened my legs under me to lessen the shock. I do not know how far I fell, but when I came to, my

were around me, I was on a bed of sand, and all around were big rocks detached from the cliff above. On examination it was found that my left arm was broken, and my feet and legs so bruised that I could not walk. One man went back to camp for a sail, from which a litter was made, and I was carried to camp.
attempted to set my arm, but it remains till now a little crooked, and weaker than the other. My feet and ankles, though bloodshot for awhile [sic], in time became as sound as ever.
